1.1.1 Objectives
The main objective of the H2020-NMBP-TO-IND-2018-2020 project is to develop a new technology to detect and quantify food fraud and contamination in real time in a smart manufacturing line for the dairy, meat and fish industries. This will be done by developing a new Near Infrared (NIR) instrument and a novel data analysis approach for food quality control. The NIR spectrometer will be integrated in a smart manufacturing line where it will be tested and validated.
The main results will be: a prototype of a novel NIR instrument, a novel data analysis approach, and a demonstration of the NIR instrument in a smart manufacturing line.
The novel NIR instrument will be based on an array of up to 15 micro-opto-electro-mechanical system (MOEMS) filters, and a photovoltaic photodetector array. The instrument will be integrated in a smart manufacturing line for the dairy, meat and fish industries. The NIR instrument will be connected with the manufacturing line control system, and the AI-based data analysis system, and feedback control will be implemented. The NIR instrument will be tested and validated in a smart manufacturing line for the dairy, meat and fish industries. In addition, the NIR instrument will be used to collect a database for NIR food quality control.
The novel data analysis approach will be based on AI and deep learning techniques. The AI-based data analysis system will be connected with the manufacturing line control system, and feedback control will be implemented. The AI-based data analysis system will be tested and validated.
The project will be carried out by a consortium of five partners from four different European countries. The consortium comprises a photonics technology developer (IRIS), a photonics components developer (ALEN), a software developer (CARTIF), and two food producers (COMITÉ DE LECHE, and PESCANOVA).

### 3.1.1 Methodology and approach

The use of NIR spectroscopy for real-time food quality control in smart manufacturing lines is a highly promising and innovative approach. The methodology we propose is based on the acquisition of high-quality spectra from food products or raw materials, which are then analyzed using chemometric algorithms to extract relevant information about the chemical composition, physical properties, and safety of the products. The approach is designed to be non-destructive, rapid, and non-invasive, enabling continuous monitoring of food quality throughout the manufacturing process.

The methodology can be divided into the following steps:

1. **Spectral acquisition:** The first step is to acquire spectra from the food products or raw materials using a portable or inline NIR spectrometer. The spectrometer is designed to cover the relevant spectral range (e.g., 800-2500 nm) and to have sufficient spectral resolution to capture the relevant features of the spectra. The spectral acquisition is performed under controlled conditions, such as temperature and humidity, to ensure the reproducibility and accuracy of the measurements.
2. **Data preprocessing:** The acquired spectra are preprocessed to remove noise, baseline drift, and other artifacts that may affect the quality of the spectra. The preprocessing techniques include smoothing, normalization, and baseline correction, among others. The preprocessing is performed using dedicated software tools, such as MATLAB or R, and is optimized for the specific application and type of spectra.
3. **Chemometric modeling:** The preprocessed spectra are used to develop chemometric models that relate the spectral features to the quality parameters of interest, such as moisture content, fat content, protein content, or microbial load. The chemometric models are developed using multivariate regression techniques, such as partial least squares regression (PLSR) or principal component regression (PCR), and are validated using independent data sets. The models are optimized for the specific application and type of spectra, and are designed to provide accurate and reliable predictions of the quality parameters.
4. **Model deployment:** The validated chemometric models are deployed in the smart manufacturing line, where the spectra are acquired in real-time using the NIR spectrometer. The spectra are preprocessed and analyzed using the chemometric models to provide real-time predictions of the quality parameters. The predictions are used to trigger alarms or to adjust the process parameters, such as temperature, pressure, or flow rate, to ensure that the quality parameters remain within the desired range.
5. **Model updating:** The chemometric models are periodically updated using new spectra and quality data acquired during the manufacturing process. The model updating is performed using adaptive algorithms, such as recursive PLS or moving window PLS, to ensure that the models remain accurate and reliable over time.

The methodology we propose is highly flexible and can be adapted to a wide range of food products and quality parameters. The approach is designed to be user-friendly and to provide actionable information to the operators and quality control personnel in the manufacturing line. The use of NIR spectroscopy for real-time food quality control in smart manufacturing lines has the potential to improve the efficiency, safety, and sustainability of the food manufacturing process, while ensuring the quality and safety of the final products.

The objective is to develop a smart manufacturing line for real-time quality control of food products (tomato, cheese, ham, and salmon) using near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy and chemometric modelling to enable fast, non-destructive, and accurate inspection of the products to achieve a high level of food safety, quality, and traceability.  

Innovation:  
The innovation lies in the development of a smart manufacturing line for real-time quality control of food products using NIR spectroscopy and chemometric modelling to achieve a high level of food safety, quality, and traceability.

- Development of a smart manufacturing line for real-time quality control of food products using NIR spectroscopy and chemometric modelling.  
- Development of a control system using the data acquired from the NIR devices to monitor the production processes and enable fast, non-destructive, and accurate inspection of the products.  
- Integration of the developed line into the production lines of the partners.

2.3 Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole  
  
2.3.1 Relevant experience, skills and infrastructure  
  
Our consortium is a balanced mix of equipment manufacturers (Polytec, Photon Systems), manufacturers of spectroscopic systems (InnospeXion, NIR4.0, IRIS), system integrators (IPT, EBC), end-users (Baader, Lysfjord, VIV, Poligrow), and research organizations (FZJ, UNIVPM, UoG, ICG). Our consortium is composed of 15 partners from 7 different EU countries, with highly complementary expertise, covering the entire value chain from research, design and development to training and dissemination. The Main activities of the partners are described below:  
The University of Portsmouth (UoP) has research expertise in developing machine learning algorithms for real-time food quality control using spectroscopic methods. In addition, UoP has expertise in the development of virtual assistants for the food industry and will lead the development of the virtual assistant in this project.  
Fraunhofer Institute for Laser Technology ILT (ILT) has many years of experience in the development of NIR and MIR laser-based process analytical spectroscopy systems for industrial applications, e.g. in the food sector. ILT will lead the development of the smart sensors and will ensure their integration into the monitoring and control systems of the processing lines.  
Fraunhofer Institute for Industrial Mathematics ITWM (ITWM) has expertise in the development of mathematical models and analysis tools for industrial processes. ITWM will develop the process optimization tools and will ensure their integration into the control systems of the processing lines.  
Fraunhofer Institute for Factory Operation and Automation IFF (IFF) has expertise in the development of virtual assistants for industrial applications. IFF will lead the development of the virtual assistant for the food industry and will ensure its integration into the monitoring and control systems of the processing lines.  
Fraunhofer Institute for Optronics, System Technologies and Image Exploitation (IOSB) has expertise in developing NIR and MIR laser-based process analytical spectroscopy systems for industrial applications, e.g. in the food sector. output: 1 1.1 Objectives and ambition 1.1.1 Objectives The overall objective of the present proposal is the development and validation of a series of miniaturised photonic sensors for the implementation of PAT in pharmaceutical manufacturing. The sensors will be based on miniaturised photonic devices and will be able to work in the demanding conditions of a manufacturing environment. The sensors will be designed to cover the main stages of the manufacturing process: raw materials, API synthesis and purification, formulation and packaging. The main sensor targets will be: (i) at raw materials level: a near infrared (NIR) imaging system for powder characterisation; (ii) at API synthesis level: an NIR probe integrated in a microfluidic system for API synthesis; (iii) at formulation level: a Raman sensor for tablet manufacturing and a NIR system for coating monitoring; and (iv) at packaging level: a miniaturised Raman system for packaging inspection. The sensors will be developed and validated in three different pharmaceutical manufacturing processes (see Figure 1): (i) API synthesis, validation of the microfluidic NIR probe; (ii) tablet manufacturing, validation of the Raman and NIR systems; and (iii) packaging, validation of the Raman system. The sensors will be tested in the manufacturing environment at three industrial sites (the synthetic process at Almirall, the formulation process at Chiesi and the packaging process at BioNTech). The main objectives are: Objective 1. Design and develop miniaturised photonic sensors for pharmaceutical PAT (M1-M24) The objective is the design and development of miniaturised photonic sensors that can be integrated in the demanding manufacturing environment. The sensors will be based on miniaturised photonic devices and will be able to work in the demanding conditions of a manufacturing environment. The sensors will be designed to cover the main stages of the manufacturing process: raw materials, API synthesis and purification, formulation and packaging. A NIR imaging system will be designed for powder characterisation, a NIR probe will be integrated in a microfluidic system for API synthesis, a Raman sensor will be designed for tablet manufacturing and a NIR system for coating monitoring, and a miniaturised Raman system will be designed for packaging inspection (see Figure 1). The sensors will be designed for integration in the manufacturing environment at three industrial sites (the synthetic process at Almirall, the formulation process at Chiesi and the packaging process at BioNTech). Objective 2. Develop efficient data analysis tools for pharmaceutical PAT (M1-M36) Data analysis tools are key for providing relevant information to pharmaceutical operators. The objective is to develop data analysis tools including calibration transfer, data fusion and machine learning algorithms. The data analysis tools will be designed for the specific photonic sensors and pharmaceutical processes. Objective 3. Test and validate the photonic sensors in pharmaceutical manufacturing (M18-M36) The objective is to demonstrate the suitability of the developed photonic sensors in three different pharmaceutical manufacturing processes: API synthesis, tablet manufacturing and packaging. The sensors will be tested in the manufacturing environment at three industrial sites (the synthetic process at Almirall, the formulation process at Chiesi and the packaging process at BioNTech). Objective 4. Define a commercialization plan (M12-M36) The objective is to define a commercialization plan for the developed photonic sensors. The commercialization plan will be developed from the beginning of the project. The commercialization plan will be based on the development of a patent strategy and the definition of a business plan. 1.1.2 Relation to the work programme and expected impact 22 The project will contribute to the work programme under cluster 4 “Digital, Industry and Space”, destination “Advanced materials, technologies and sectors”. Our technology is based on our expertise in photonic sensors and in particular in near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy. Our most relevant results in this context are:

- A Photonic NIR spectrometer for the measurements of aqueous solutions, such as water-alcohol mixtures. We designed a NIR spectrometer based on photonic components fabricated in our foundry and we demonstrated that it can measure water-alcohol mixtures with very high sensitivity and reproducibility. We also demonstrated that our NIR spectrometer can be used for machine learning models for the analysis of these mixtures (see [1]).

- An optofluidic NIR sensor for the inline analysis of liquid mixtures. We designed an optofluidic NIR sensor based on photonic components fabricated in our foundry and we demonstrated that it can measure water-acetone mixtures with very high sensitivity and reproducibility. We also demonstrated that our NIR spectrometer can be used for machine learning models for the analysis of these mixtures (see [2]).

The expected impacts of the project are:

- Improved safety and reduced risks: the project will support and contribute to the implementation of the EU strategy on Chemical Safety that aims at minimising the risks related to the use of chemicals and to improve chemical safety for consumers. The project will contribute to the achievement of the relevant chemical safety roadmaps and policy objectives, such as "The Commission strategy for a non-toxic environment", or "The Chemicals Strategy for Sustainability". This will be achieved by the project through the design of new, more efficient and safer chemical and materials production processes.

- Improved resource efficiency: the project will contribute to the transition towards a more circular economy by improving the resource efficiency of the production processes and the use and end of life of chemicals and materials.
- Increased competitiveness and Innovation: the project will contribute to the objectives of the EU Industrial Policy, and specifically to the achievement of the key objectives of the Strategic Implementation Plan of the Photonics Public Private Partnership, by supporting the development of innovative photonics-based manufacturing processes for chemical and material production, which will increase the competitiveness of the EU industry and contribute to the achievement of the targets for the photonics sector.

domain_desc: hyperspectral imaging for automated plastic waste sorting in circular economy facilities
output:
1.3.1 Objectives

The overall objective of this research is to develop a novel hyperspectral imaging system for the automated sorting of post-consumer plastic waste from non-plastic waste in circular economy facilities. This system will integrate with existing waste sorting infrastructure to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of the sorting process.

To achieve this, we set the following specific objectives:

- To develop a hyperspectral imaging system for real-time acquisition of spectral data from post-consumer plastic waste.
- To design and develop algorithms for the accurate identification and classification of different types of plastics based on hyperspectral data.
- To integrate the hyperspectral imaging system with existing mechanical sorting mechanisms for automated sorting.
- To validate the performance of the developed system in a real circular economy facility.

1.3.2 Research questions

The research questions guiding this study are:

- How can hyperspectral imaging be optimized for the identification of post-consumer plastic waste in circular economy facilities?
- What algorithms are most effective for the classification of different types of plastics using hyperspectral data?
- How can the developed hyperspectral imaging system be seamlessly integrated with existing waste sorting infrastructure?
- What is the impact of the automated sorting system on the overall efficiency and accuracy of the waste sorting process in circular economy facilities?

1.3.3 State-of-the-art

The current state-of-the-art in waste sorting primarily relies on manual sorting and conventional optical sorting technologies, such as near-infrared (NIR) spectroscopy and X-ray fluorescence (XRF) spectroscopy. These technologies offer some level of automation but are limited in their ability to accurately identify and sort complex waste streams, particularly in post-consumer plastic waste.

Recent advancements in hyperspectral imaging technology have shown promise in addressing these limitations. Hyperspectral imaging captures spectral information across a wide range of wavelengths, providing detailed information about the material composition of objects. This technology has been successfully applied in various fields, including agriculture, food processing, and environmental monitoring, for material identification and classification.

In the context of waste sorting, hyperspectral imaging has started to gain attention for its potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of sorting processes. Studies have demonstrated the capability of hyperspectral imaging to differentiate between various types of plastics, including those that are challenging to identify using conventional techniques. However, there is a lack of integrated systems that combine hyperspectral imaging with automated sorting mechanisms in circular economy facilities.

Our research aims to bridge this gap by developing a comprehensive hyperspectral imaging system specifically designed for the automated sorting of post-consumer plastic waste in circular economy facilities. By leveraging the strengths of hyperspectral imaging and integrating it with existing sorting infrastructure, we aim to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of the waste sorting process, thereby contributing to the advancement of circular economy practices.

1.3.4 Methodology and approach

Work conducted in this section will encompass:

- **Task 1.1:** Definition of user requirements and system specifications.
- **Task 1.2:** Development of a hyperspectral imaging system.
- **Task 1.3:** Design and development of classification algorithms for plastic waste.
- **Task 1.4:** Integration of the imaging system with mechanical sorting mechanisms.
- **Task 1.5:** Validation of the system in a real circular economy facility.

**Task 1.1: Definition of user requirements and system specifications**

Objective: To gather and analyze user requirements for the hyperspectral imaging system and define the system specifications accordingly.

Approach: We will conduct interviews and workshops with stakeholders, including facility operators, waste management experts, and technology providers, to understand the specific needs and constraints of circular economy facilities. Based on this information, we will define the technical specifications for the hyperspectral imaging system, including spectral range, resolution, acquisition speed, and integration requirements.

**Task 1.2: Development of a hyperspectral imaging system**

Objective: To design and develop a hyperspectral imaging system tailored for the identification of post-consumer plastic waste.

Approach: We will select appropriate hyperspectral sensors and optics based on the defined specifications. The system will be designed to capture spectral data in the relevant wavelength range for plastic identification. We will develop a data acquisition setup that ensures real-time imaging capabilities and is compatible with the operational environment of circular economy facilities.

**Task 1.3: Design and development of classification algorithms for plastic waste**

Objective: To develop algorithms capable of accurately identifying and classifying different types of plastics based on hyperspectral data.

Approach: We will collect a comprehensive dataset of hyperspectral images of various plastic materials. Using this dataset, we will design and train machine learning algorithms, such as support vector machines (SVM) and convolutional neural networks (CNN), to classify plastics based on their spectral signatures. The algorithms will be optimized for real-time processing and accuracy.

**Task 1.4: Integration of the imaging system with mechanical sorting mechanisms**

Objective: To seamlessly integrate the hyperspectral imaging system with existing mechanical sorting mechanisms for automated sorting.

1.3.1 We will produce a novel HSI system with a complete set of components adapted to the specific needs of plastic sorting. This system will be entirely based on the integration of photonic components and will be composed of the following main components:

- Several miniaturized spectrometers to cover the complete spectral range of interest.
- Optical components to distribute the light to the different spectrometers.
- An illumination system based on LEDs.
- High-speed electronics adapted to the high data flow generated by the spectrometers.
- A real-time data processing system.
- A real-time data visualization system.

The miniaturized spectrometers will be based on a novel 2D photodetector array. The dispersive element will be based on a multi-layer dielectric (MLD) coating. This coating is composed of alternating layers of materials with different refractive indexes (RIs). The number of layers is high enough to create an interference effect. This interference effect causes a specific wavelength to be reflected and all the other wavelengths to be transmitted, or vice versa. The wavelength that is filtered out is called the central wavelength and it depends on the thickness and refractive indexes of the layers. This interference effect can be used to create an optical filter. The wavelength that is filtered out can be modified by modifying the angle of incidence (AOI) of the light on the optical filter. The MLD coating will be applied on top of the photodetector array. The photodetector array will be based on CMOS technology. The photodetector array and the MLD coating will be optimized to achieve a spectral resolution of 8 nm. To cover the full NIR spectral range, we will use 5 spectrometers with independent optical paths. The optical path will be optimized to ensure that the AOI of the light on the MLD is sufficiently different for each of the spectrometers.

The illumination system will be based on LEDs. LEDs will be chosen to cover the full NIR spectral range. LEDs will be optimized to provide the highest optical power possible. The LEDs will be arranged to ensure a uniform illumination of the plastic objects. The LEDs will be divided into several groups. Each group will be controlled independently. The lighting system will be optimized to maximize the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the HSI system. The lighting system will be controlled in real-time based on the results of the data processing system. This control will allow us to optimize the SNR.

The data processing system will be based on a set of algorithms able to extract the most relevant features from the HSI data. It will be optimized to run on the edge. The data processing system will allow us to extract the most relevant features from the HSI data. The data processing system will be composed of two parts: an edge part and a cloud part. The edge part will allow us to extract the most relevant features from the HSI data. The cloud part will allow us to perform more complex data processing.

- The use of a novel 2D photodetector array with a MLD coating.

1.1. Impact of the project  
The HYPERSPECTRUM project will deliver new hyperspectral imaging technologies that will empower the circular economy of plastic waste.  
The new hyperspectral imaging technologies will be deployed to circular economy facilities to enable automated plastic waste sorting, which will increase their efficiency by 20% (compared to current manual sorting) and reduce their operation costs by 15%. Furthermore, the new sorting technologies will increase the recycled plastic quality by 10%.  
The HYPERSPECTRUM project will contribute to the creation of new EU companies (5) and new jobs (around 50) directly linked to the manufacturing and deployment of the new hyperspectral imaging systems. Furthermore, the project will create new jobs (around 200) in circular economy facilities due to the increased waste processing.  
The HYPERSPECTRUM project will contribute to the sustainability of the EU by reducing the amount of plastic waste sent to landfills and incinerators, which will have a positive impact on the circular economy of plastic waste and will create a cleaner and healthier environment.  
1.2. Measures to maximise impact  
1.2.1. Dissemination and exploitation of results  
We will continuously monitor the dissemination and exploitation activities to ensure the maximum impact of the project. We will submit the results of our research to scientific journals and present them at conferences to engage with the scientific community. Our involvement in the Photonic21 association will help us reach a broader audience. Furthermore, we will exploit the results of the project through the commercialization of our new hyperspectral imaging systems.  
1.2.2. Communication activities  
We will implement a communication plan to increase awareness and understanding of the project among the general public. The plan will include the creation of a user-friendly website, the publication of articles in newspapers and magazines, the organization of workshops and demonstrations in circular economy facilities, and the participation in local and international events. The website will be updated regularly with the results of our research and the exploitation of the project.  
1.3. Summary of the project’s deliverables and milestones  
The project will generate 30 deliverables and 36 milestones. The most important deliverables are: D3.2 (demonstrator #1), D3.3 (demonstrator #2), D3.4 (demonstrator #3), and D4.3 (commercial hyperspectral imaging system). The most important milestones are: MS1 (data gathering completed), MS12 (demonstrator #1 completed), MS13 (demonstrator #2 completed), and MS14 (demonstrator #3 completed).  
1.4. Summary of the costs and the funding required for the project  
The total cost of the project is 3,063,672.02 €. The requested EU contribution is 2,999,820.62 €. The total person-months required for the project are 28.9, 13.0, 12.9, 13.4, 13.0, 12.9, and 12.9 for WP1, WP2, WP3, WP4, WP5, WP6, and WP7, respectively.  
The HYPERSPECTRUM project has a 36-month duration and was submitted to the NMBP-22-2020 call.
